title: Lesson 388
transcript: Let's now look at how actual traffic is sent across networks. And so this is referred to in network terminology as transport architecture, right? We've already defined words like architecture But in this case, we're talking about transport. How does, for example, network traffic actually move from device to device across a network? And the first thing that we need to understand is the word topology. In fact, if you look at the word topology and look it up in a dictionary, It's going to say how something is mapped. And so if you remember what a network actually is, it's just a whole bunch of devices that are connected to each other. How we logically and physically connect those devices together is referred to as its topology or network topology. And we'll look at this in more detail. But basically, a topology is how something is actually mapped, how the devices that make up our network are actually connected to each other physically and logically is referred to as the topology. Now, each device that makes up a network, for example, has to be able to handle traffic. And so if you think of devices as having different logical constructs, one of those logical constructs that it requires is something called the data plane. And you can think of the data plane as the construct that actually moves the frames. So let's take a switch as an example. a network switch, right? A network switch when it encounters frames, it actually has to move that information through the switch. That's what the data plane does. It actually makes and forwards the traffic based on decisions that have actually been made by something called the control plane, which we'll describe in more detail in a few minutes here. But think of the data plane as the low-level component of a network device that actually forwards packets and frames and so on, right? That's what the data plane is considered to be. It's the doer, basically. And it can do it in two ways. That's what cut through switching and store and forward switching is all about. So there's two ways that the data plane can actually move those frames. if we're using a switch as an example here, right? And the difference between cut through and store and forward is very simple. Cut and through starts moving the frame as soon as it understands or receives the destination address. It doesn't have to wait for the entire frame, for example, because it can start moving that traffic as soon as it understands the destination address. So this would be more efficient and faster, but the problem with this is that it cannot do error correction, which is what store and forward, as these words imply here, store and forward will store the entire frame, and look at the destination address, but move that frame only after it's done some error checking. Because if we store the entire frame, for example, then we can look at it, we can do some error checking, and then if everything's okay, then we start moving it. That's the difference. Obviously, store and forward would introduce a little more latency, because we have to store that frame first. before we can actually move it. Whereas in cut and through, as soon as we have a few bytes where we understand the destination address, we can start moving it. So cut and through, is faster, more efficient, but doesn't do anything related to error correction. Where store and forward does error correction, but introduces a bit more latency. So those are the two differences. So from a security perspective, right, because we always have to focus on security when we describe anything related to, in this case, networks and network security. Obviously, the integrity of transmissions is something that store and forward could give us. but we're gonna pay for it in efficiency. Whereas cutting through is very efficient, but doesn't provide any sort of integrity of the transmission or error correction of the transmission itself. Now, just to complete the picture of what we're talking about here, which is transport architecture. The data plane is what actually moves the traffic and the information, but it can only do that once it has received instructions from something called the control plane. So these are the two different logical constructs that every device, for example, network device needs to be able to logically separate. So the way that you can think of these is that the control plane makes the decisions and then the data plane will act on those decisions and actually do and move the traffic across those devices. So these are interconnected, obviously, but you can think of the control plane as what makes a decision and the data plane forwards the actual information based on the decisions that were made by the control plane. Then we can take this to another higher level as well, and that is to talk about what we call the management plane. A key word, obviously, management. but it makes decisions for the entire network from a logical perspective. And certainly, these constructs that we have here have given growth to software-defined networking that we're going to talk about later. in this presentation as well. But the management plane, as you can see here, its purpose is to configure, manage, maintain, and do all kinds of things related to the management of the entire network itself. and then of course the control plane will make the decisions that it will pass to the data plane that will actually act on those decisions that were being made by the control plane itself.
